title: Monte Carlo simulation for tolerance analysis (Level 3)
description:
Implement Monte Carlo simulation for statistical tolerance analysis.

## Goal
Run statistical simulations to understand yield probability, not just worst-case.

## File Location
packages/temper-placer/src/temper_placer/manufacturing/monte_carlo.py

## Concept
Level 3 uses Monte Carlo simulation to:
- Sample manufacturing parameters from distributions
- Run DRC on each sample
- Report yield probability (% of samples passing)
- Identify most likely failure modes

## Data Structures

from dataclasses import dataclass
import jax.numpy as jnp
import jax.random as random

@dataclass
class DistributionParams:
    '''Parameters for a tolerance distribution'''
    mean: float
    std_dev: float
    distribution: str = 'normal'  # 'normal', 'uniform', 'truncated_normal'
    min_val: float = None
    max_val: float = None

@dataclass
class ManufacturingVariables:
    '''All manufacturing parameters that vary'''
    etch_tolerance: DistributionParams
    drill_tolerance: DistributionParams
    registration_x: DistributionParams
    registration_y: DistributionParams
    copper_thickness: DistributionParams
    dielectric_thickness: DistributionParams

@dataclass
class MonteCarloConfig:
    num_samples: int = 1000
    seed: int = 42
    parallel: bool = True
    report_percentiles: list[float] = (0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 0.9, 0.99)

@dataclass
class MonteCarloResult:
    '''Results of Monte Carlo simulation'''
    num_samples: int
    num_passing: int
    yield_probability: float
    
    # Per-rule statistics
    rule_violations: dict[str, int]  # rule -> count of violations
    
    # Clearance statistics
    min_clearance_distribution: jnp.ndarray
    percentiles: dict[str, float]
    
    # Most common failure modes
    failure_modes: list[tuple[str, float]]  # (mode, frequency)

class MonteCarloSimulator:
    '''Run Monte Carlo tolerance simulation'''
    
    def __init__(
        self,
        variables: ManufacturingVariables,
        config: MonteCarloConfig = MonteCarloConfig()
    ):
        self.variables = variables
        self.config = config
        self.key = random.PRNGKey(config.seed)
    
    def sample_parameters(self, n: int) -> dict[str, jnp.ndarray]:
        '''Generate n samples of all manufacturing parameters'''
        samples = {}
        
        for name, params in vars(self.variables).items():
            if params is None:
                continue
            
            self.key, subkey = random.split(self.key)
            
            if params.distribution == 'normal':
                samples[name] = random.normal(
                    subkey, shape=(n,)
                ) * params.std_dev + params.mean
            elif params.distribution == 'uniform':
                samples[name] = random.uniform(
                    subkey, shape=(n,),
                    minval=params.min_val,
                    maxval=params.max_val
                )
        
        return samples
    
    def run_simulation(
        self,
        placement: PlacementState,
        drc_rules: DRCRules
    ) -> MonteCarloResult:
        '''Run Monte Carlo simulation'''
        samples = self.sample_parameters(self.config.num_samples)
        
        # Vectorized DRC check
        passing = []
        violations = {rule: 0 for rule in drc_rules.rules}
        
        for i in range(self.config.num_samples):
            # Apply this sample's tolerances
            inflated_rules = self._apply_sample(drc_rules, samples, i)
            
            # Run DRC
            result = check_drc(placement, inflated_rules)
            
            if result.passed:
                passing.append(True)
            else:
                passing.append(False)
                for violation in result.violations:
                    violations[violation.rule] += 1
        
        yield_prob = sum(passing) / len(passing)
        
        # Find most common failure modes
        failure_modes = sorted(
            [(k, v/self.config.num_samples) for k, v in violations.items()],
            key=lambda x: -x[1]
        )[:5]
        
        return MonteCarloResult(
            num_samples=self.config.num_samples,
            num_passing=sum(passing),
            yield_probability=yield_prob,
            rule_violations=violations,
            failure_modes=failure_modes,
            ...
        )

## Usage

simulator = MonteCarloSimulator(
    variables=ManufacturingVariables(
        etch_tolerance=DistributionParams(mean=0.05, std_dev=0.01),
        drill_tolerance=DistributionParams(mean=0.1, std_dev=0.02),
        ...
    ),
    config=MonteCarloConfig(num_samples=10000)
)

result = simulator.run_simulation(placement, drc_rules)
print(f'Yield: {result.yield_probability:.1%}')
print(f'Top failure: {result.failure_modes[0]}')

## Tests Required
- tests/manufacturing/test_monte_carlo.py:
  - Test parameter sampling
  - Test simulation runs
  - Test yield calculation
  - Test failure mode identification

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Samples from configurable distributions
- [ ] Reports yield probability
- [ ] Identifies top failure modes
- [ ] JAX-accelerated sampling
- [ ] Configurable number of samples

title: Heuristic: Functional Module Clustering
description:
Implement functional module clustering for initial placement.

## Problem
Components that work together (same schematic sheet, same functional block) should start near each other.

## Approach
1. Parse schematic hierarchy from netlist (sheet names, reference prefixes)
2. Identify functional modules:
   - Gate driver module: U_GATE, R_GATE1, R_GATE2, C_BOOT
   - Buck module: U_BUCK, L_BUCK, C_IN, C_OUT
   - MCU module: U_MCU, C_DEC1, Y1 (crystal)
   - Sensing module: U_TEMP, R_SENSE
3. Compute centroid for each module
4. Place module members around centroid with small random offset

## Implementation
- Add `identify_functional_modules(netlist)` function
- Add `cluster_placement(modules, board)` function
- Integrate into `PlacementState.smart_init()`

## Acceptance Criteria
- Modules identified from netlist structure or explicit config
- Components within a module start within 15mm of each other
- Modules spread across board (not all in center)

title: Integrate SpectralInitializer with optimizer
description:
Create SpectralInitializer class and integrate with optimizer pipeline.

## Interface
```python
class SpectralInitializer:
    """Initialize component positions using spectral graph layout."""
    
    def __init__(
        self,
        normalized_laplacian: bool = True,
        margin_fraction: float = 0.1,
        handle_disconnected: str = "separate",  # or "random"
    ):
        ...
    
    def initialize(
        self,
        netlist: Netlist,
        board: Board,
        rng_key: Optional[jax.random.PRNGKey] = None,
    ) -> jnp.ndarray:
        """
        Compute initial positions for all components.
        
        Args:
            netlist: Components and connectivity
            board: Board dimensions
            rng_key: For any randomness (e.g., disconnected components)
            
        Returns:
            (N, 2) initial positions
        """
```

## Integration Points

### 1. Optimizer Config
```yaml
optimizer:
  initialization:
    strategy: spectral  # new option
    params:
      normalized: true
      margin: 0.1
```

### 2. Optimizer Factory
```python
def create_optimizer(config: OptimizerConfig, netlist: Netlist, board: Board):
    if config.initialization.strategy == "spectral":
        initializer = SpectralInitializer(**config.initialization.params)
    elif config.initialization.strategy == "random":
        initializer = RandomInitializer(**config.initialization.params)
    ...
    
    initial_positions = initializer.initialize(netlist, board)
```

### 3. CLI Flag
```bash
temper-placer optimize --init spectral board.kicad_pcb
```

## Handling Disconnected Components
If graph has multiple connected components:
1. **separate**: Run spectral on each, tile results
2. **random**: Place disconnected components randomly
3. **heuristic**: Place by component type/function

## Testing
- End-to-end: load netlist → spectral init → verify positions valid
- Compare initial wirelength: spectral vs random
- Verify config loading works
- CLI flag works

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SpectralInitializer class complete
- [ ] Config schema updated with `initialization.strategy`
- [ ] Optimizer uses configured initializer
- [ ] CLI `--init` flag added
- [ ] Disconnected component handling works
- [ ] Integration tests pass

title: Implement ViaDensityLoss
description:
Implement loss function to estimate and minimize required vias based on placement.

## Problem
Placement affects routing complexity and via count:
- Components on opposite sides of board need vias to connect
- Layer transitions (power planes, signal layers) require vias
- High via density increases cost and reduces reliability
- Via placement affects signal integrity (inductance, impedance discontinuity)

## Concept
Without actual routing, estimate via needs from placement:
1. Nets that span far across the board likely need layer changes
2. Crossing signal paths imply via usage
3. Power delivery to distant components needs via fields

## Simplified Model

```python
class ViaDensityLoss(Loss):
    """Estimate via requirements from placement."""
    
    def __init__(
        self,
        via_cost: float = 0.01,  # Cost per estimated via
        crossing_weight: float = 1.0,
        span_weight: float = 0.5,
    ):
        self.via_cost = via_cost
        self.crossing_weight = crossing_weight
        self.span_weight = span_weight
    
    def __call__(self, positions, rotations, context) -> LossResult:
        total_vias = 0.0
        
        # Estimate 1: Net span (long nets need layer changes)
        for net_name, comp_indices in context.net_to_components.items():
            if len(comp_indices) < 2:
                continue
            net_positions = positions[comp_indices]
            span = self._compute_net_span(net_positions)
            # Heuristic: 1 via per 20mm of span
            total_vias += self.span_weight * span / 20.0
        
        # Estimate 2: Signal crossings
        crossings = self._estimate_crossings(positions, context)
        total_vias += self.crossing_weight * crossings
        
        return LossResult(value=self.via_cost * total_vias)
    
    def _compute_net_span(self, positions):
        """Compute bounding box diagonal of net components."""
        min_xy = jnp.min(positions, axis=0)
        max_xy = jnp.max(positions, axis=0)
        return jnp.sqrt(jnp.sum((max_xy - min_xy) ** 2))
    
    def _estimate_crossings(self, positions, context):
        """Estimate number of net-to-net crossings."""
        # Simplified: count intersecting bounding boxes of nets
        # More sophisticated: check actual line segment crossings
        pass
```

## Crossing Detection
For each pair of nets, check if their "routing corridors" intersect:
- Corridor = convex hull of component positions for that net
- Intersection implies routing conflict → likely via

## Limitations
- Very rough estimate - actual via count depends on routing
- Does not account for power plane vias (would need stackup info)
- Better for relative comparison than absolute prediction

## Testing
- Compact placement → low via estimate
- Spread placement → high via estimate
- Many crossing nets → higher estimate
- Compare estimate vs actual via count after routing (calibration)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Provides reasonable relative ranking of placements
- Fast computation (<50ms for 200 components)
- Configurable weights for different via estimation methods
- Works as soft loss in multi-objective optimization

## Priority
Low - This is a secondary optimization metric. Focus on primary losses first.

title: Implement Netlist Community Detection (Louvain)

title: Heuristic: Keep-out Zone Awareness
description:
Respect keep-out zones during initial placement to avoid wasted optimization.

## Problem
PCBs have keep-out zones where components cannot be placed:
- Mounting holes with clearance rings
- Heatsink mounting areas
- Connector mating clearance
- Under-board component clearance (if using both sides)
- Regulatory/safety clearance zones (high voltage isolation)

Random initialization places components in keep-outs, wasting epochs moving them out.

## Approach
1. Parse keep-out zones from board definition (KiCad zones, explicit config)
2. Create placement mask (valid vs invalid regions)
3. During initialization, only place in valid regions
4. Add buffer zone around keep-outs for safety margin

## Keep-out Sources
- KiCad: Zone objects with "keepout" type
- Config YAML: Explicit rectangle/polygon definitions
- Auto-generated: Mounting hole clearance (typically 3-5mm radius)
- Auto-generated: Board edge clearance (typically 0.5-1mm)

## Implementation
- Add `parse_keepout_zones(board, config)` function
- Add `create_placement_mask(keepouts, board_bounds, resolution)` function
- Add `valid_random_position(mask, component_size)` function
- Integrate into `PlacementState.smart_init()`

## Example for Temper
Keep-out zones:
- 4x mounting holes: 5mm clearance radius each
- High-voltage area: 10mm clearance from low-voltage
- Heatsink footprint: 30x50mm at top center
- Edge clearance: 1mm from all board edges

## Acceptance Criteria
- No components initialized inside keep-out zones
- Components respect buffer distance from keep-out edges
- Keep-outs parsed from KiCad board file automatically
- Override/additional keep-outs via config YAML

title: Fix ClearanceLoss for DRC Parity
description:
## Status: BLOCKED - Architectural Limitation

The original goal of achieving zero DRC errors on external PCBs is **impossible without a router**.

### Root Cause Analysis

After extensive testing, we discovered:

1. **minimal_board tests pass** - Our fixture has no pre-routed traces, so component movement doesn't break connectivity

2. **External PCB tests cannot pass** - These PCBs have:
   - Pre-routed traces connecting components at their original positions
   - Vias placed for the original layout
   - Any component movement breaks trace connections
   - KiCad DRC reports these broken connections as errors

### What ClearanceLoss Actually Does (Correctly)

The ClearanceLoss implementation is **working correctly**:
- margin parameter adds clearance buffer to overlap detection
- rotation_invariant mode uses max(width, height) for safe bounds
- High weight (1000x) makes it a hard constraint

### Why External PCBs Fail DRC

When we move components on external PCBs:
1. Traces that connected to original pad positions now connect to empty space
2. This creates 'items shorting two nets' or 'unconnected items' errors
3. These are **routing errors, not placement errors**

### Path Forward

To optimize pre-routed PCBs, we need one of:

**Option A: Strip traces before optimization**
- Remove all traces/vias from external PCBs
- Optimize placement only
- Re-route manually or with external router
- This loses the human design intent

**Option B: Add routing capability (significant scope)**
- Implement basic autorouter
- Optimize placement + routing together
- Major architectural addition

**Option C: Limit scope to unrouted PCBs**
- temper-placer is a PLACEMENT optimizer
- External PCB DRC tests are out of scope
- Focus on generating good initial placements for manual routing

### Recommendation

Close this task as 'wont-fix' or 'out-of-scope'. The ClearanceLoss is working correctly for its intended purpose (preventing overlap/clearance violations). The DRC failures on external PCBs are routing errors, not placement errors.

Create a new epic for 'Router Integration' if full PCB optimization is desired.

title: TDD: Ensure optimizer produces zero-DRC-error placements
description:
Create a TDD test suite that ensures optimized placements have zero DRC errors.

Current state:
- Perfect (hand-crafted) placements: 0 errors, 4 warnings (lib config)
- Optimized placements: 9-10 errors (clearance, shorting, solder mask bridge)

Root cause: Optimizer minimizes wirelength by pushing components close together, causing clearance violations.

TDD approach:
1. test_optimized_placement_zero_drc_errors - MUST pass
2. test_optimizer_respects_clearance_rules - Add clearance loss
3. test_convergence_maintains_drc_compliance - Check throughout optimization

Implementation options:
a) Add ClearanceLoss that penalizes components closer than DRC minimum
b) Increase overlap penalty weight to discourage close placement
c) Add hard constraint for minimum component spacing

Success criteria: An optimized placement (400 epochs) has 0 DRC errors.

title: Firmware HAL Abstraction and TDD Infrastructure
description:
Implement Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) and Test-Driven Development infrastructure for the Temper induction cooker firmware. This enables unit testing of all control logic (state machine, PID, safety, pan detection, PLL) without hardware dependencies.

**Goals:**
1. Abstract all hardware access through HAL interfaces (GPIO, ADC, PWM, SPI, Timer)
2. Create mock implementations for host-based testing
3. Implement comprehensive unit tests using ESP-IDF Unity framework
4. Verify firmware behavior against design specifications

**Key Specifications to Test Against:**
- OCP threshold: 50A ±5A (2.5V at comparator)
- OVP threshold: 390V (3.55V after divider)
- Thermal shutdown: 85°C IGBT junction
- Watchdog timeout: 1.6s
- Temperature precision: ±0.5°C
- Switching frequency: 38-50 kHz
- Dead-time: 500ns minimum
- Pan detection: valid 3-15A, no-pan >20A, small object <3A

**Deliverables:**
- HAL interface headers in firmware/components/hal/include/
- Mock implementations in firmware/components/hal/mock/
- ESP32 implementations in firmware/components/hal/esp32/
- Test suite in firmware/test/
- All tests passing against design specifications

title: CRITICAL-1: Upgrade Resonant Capacitor to ≥700V Rating
description:
## Problem
The resonant capacitor is already operating above its voltage rating - this is a FIRE HAZARD.

| Parameter | Specified | Simulated | Margin |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Capacitor voltage rating | 630V | 648V peak | -2.9% |

## Failure Scenario
1. Capacitor operates above rated voltage every switching cycle
2. Dielectric stress causes gradual degradation
3. Internal short or venting occurs
4. Fire or explosion in enclosed chassis

## Required Actions

### Immediate
- Update BOM with capacitor rated ≥700V (50% margin over 648V peak)
- Select film capacitor with appropriate ripple current rating (≥15A RMS at 38kHz)
- Verify physical dimensions fit existing layout
- Update simulation with new capacitor parameters

### Recommended Part Selection Criteria
- Voltage Rating: ≥700V DC (prefer 800V or 1000V)
- Capacitance: Match existing value (likely 100nF-470nF based on resonant freq)
- Dielectric: Polypropylene film (PP) - best for high-frequency, high-voltage
- Ripple Current: ≥15A RMS at 38kHz
- Package: Radial or axial, check mechanical fit

### Candidate Parts (verify availability/specs)
- KEMET R76 series (1000V DC)
- Vishay MKP series (800-1000V)
- EPCOS B32774 series (800V)
- Cornell Dubilier 940C series (800V)

## Verification
- Simulation confirms peak voltage with 20% margin to new rating
- Thermal analysis confirms adequate cooling
- Physical fit verified in mechanical design

## Risk if Not Fixed
**Cannot ship hardware. Liability exposure. Potential kitchen fire.**

title: Fix critical ato vs schematic discrepancies

title: atopile: 'assert X is Y' syntax not supported in v0.2.69
description:
The 'assert X is Y' syntax for explicit equation verification does not exist in atopile 0.2.69.

**Evidence:**
- Building with 'assert t_dead_time is 5ns * (dt_cap.value / 1nF)' causes SyntaxError
- Error: 'mismatched input 'is' expecting...'
- Checked atopile GitHub issues - no 'is' assertion syntax exists

**Workaround:**
Use 'assert X within Y to Z' for range assertions instead.

**Impact:**
- Blocks temper-ofq.3 (equation assertions)
- Cannot verify calculated values match formulas at compile time

**Request:**
Consider filing upstream feature request for 'is' syntax to enable equation verification.

title: REQ-ELEC-03: Via Specification and Current Rating
description:
Define via types, sizes, and current ratings for all PCB requirements.

## Via Types Required

### 1. Standard Signal Via
- **Drill:** 0.3mm (12 mil)
- **Pad:** 0.6mm (24 mil)
- **Annular Ring:** 0.15mm (6 mil)
- **Current Rating:** 0.5A per via
- **Usage:** Signal layer transitions

### 2. Power Via
- **Drill:** 0.5mm (20 mil)
- **Pad:** 1.0mm (40 mil)
- **Annular Ring:** 0.25mm (10 mil)
- **Current Rating:** 1.5A per via
- **Usage:** Power rail distribution (5V, 3.3V)

### 3. High-Current Via
- **Drill:** 0.6mm (24 mil)
- **Pad:** 1.2mm (48 mil)
- **Annular Ring:** 0.3mm (12 mil)
- **Current Rating:** 2.5A per via
- **Usage:** DC bus, IGBT power paths

### 4. Thermal Via
- **Drill:** 0.4mm (16 mil)
- **Pad:** 0.8mm (32 mil)
- **Annular Ring:** 0.2mm (8 mil)
- **Current Rating:** N/A (thermal only)
- **Thermal Resistance:** ~70°C/W per via (filled)
- **Usage:** Thermal pad connections, heatsinking

## Via Array Requirements

### DC Bus Via Array
- **Total Current:** 22A peak
- **Via Type:** High-Current (2.5A each)
- **Minimum Count:** 22/2.5 = 9 vias, use 12 minimum
- **Pattern:** 3×4 array, 2mm pitch

### IGBT Pad Via Array
- **Purpose:** Thermal + electrical
- **Via Type:** Thermal (0.4mm)
- **Count:** 20+ under each TO-247 pad
- **Pattern:** Grid fill, 1mm pitch
- **Note:** May require via-in-pad with fill

### Buck Converter Thermal Pad
- **Via Type:** Thermal
- **Count:** 6-9 vias under exposed pad
- **Pattern:** 3×3 array

## Deliverables
- [ ] Via specification table in PCB_SPECIFICATION.md
- [ ] KiCad via size presets configured
- [ ] Via array templates created
- [ ] Current derating curves documented

## Acceptance Criteria
- Via arrays sized for >50% safety margin on current
- Thermal vias properly connected to ground planes
- No unfilled via-in-pad on BGA/QFN (if used)

title: IKW40N120H3: Footprint name doesn't match KiCad standard library
description:
## Issue
The IKW40N120H3 component in `elec/src/components.ato` uses a non-standard footprint name.

## Current:
```
footprint = "IGBT_TO247"
```

## Recommended:
```
footprint = "Package_TO_SOT_THT:TO-247-3_Vertical"
```

## Evidence
The KiCad symbol file (`components/IKW40N120H3/IKW40N120H3.kicad_sym` line 9) uses:
```
(property "Footprint" "Package_TO_SOT_THT:TO-247-3_Vertical" ...
```

## Impact
**Low** - May cause footprint lookup issues if a custom `IGBT_TO247` footprint doesn't exist. Using the standard KiCad library footprint name ensures compatibility.

## Fix
Edit `elec/src/components.ato` line 12 to use the standard KiCad footprint name.

title: Implement Subgraph Partitioning in SpectralInitializer
description:
Problem: find_connected_components() exists (initialization.py:96-130) but is never called. Disjoint subgraphs get placed on top of each other because spectral coordinates are normalized together.

Root Cause: SpectralInitializer.initialize() (line 226) processes all components as single graph, relying on flawed assumption that multiple zero eigenvalues naturally separate disjoint subgraphs. They dont - they collapse to center.

Implementation Steps:
1. Modify SpectralInitializer.initialize() to call find_connected_components()
2. Add _initialize_disjoint_subgraphs() method that:
   - Sorts components by size (largest first)
   - Computes spectral coords for each subgraph independently
   - Packs subgraphs: largest at center, smaller in corners/spiral
   - Returns scaled positions for all components
3. Add _pack_subgraphs() helper using bin-packing or grid layout

TDD Tests (add to test_initialization.py):
- test_disjoint_two_islands: C1-C2 and C3-C4, verify centers >20mm apart
- test_disjoint_three_subgraphs: power + digital + analog domains
- test_single_isolated_component: verify goes to corner, not center
- test_eigenvalue_computation_disjoint: verify no crash on disconnected graph

Acceptance: All tests pass, disjoint graphs dont overlap after spectral init.
Action After Complete: Close temper-d5x.

title: Refactor RTDSensing to use SPI and ElectricPower interfaces

This is the simplest module to migrate (leaf node, single SPI peripheral).

title: Electrical Design Rules
description:
Sub-epic covering all electrical design rules for the Temper PCB, including net classes, trace width calculations, via specifications, clearance rules, and current capacity requirements.

## Scope

title: REQ-DFM-01: Component Placement for Pick-and-Place
description:
Define component placement rules for automated pick-and-place assembly.

## SMT Component Placement Rules

### General Orientation
- **ICs:** Pin 1 towards top-left or consistent orientation
- **Passives:** Consistent 0° or 90° orientation per type
- **Polarized:** Consistent polarity direction where possible

### Component Spacing

#### Minimum Spacing (SMD to SMD)
| Package | Min Pad-to-Pad | Recommended |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 0402 | 0.15mm | 0.25mm |
| 0603 | 0.20mm | 0.30mm |
| 0805 | 0.25mm | 0.35mm |
| SOIC | 0.25mm | 0.40mm |
| QFN | 0.25mm | 0.40mm |
| Module (ESP32) | 1.0mm | 2.0mm |

#### SMD to Through-Hole Spacing
- Minimum: 1.0mm from SMD pad edge to THT pad edge
- Recommended: 2.0mm for rework access

### Component Orientation by Type

#### Resistors/Capacitors (0603/0805)
- Orientation: 0° (horizontal) or 90° (vertical)
- Consistent within each board area
- Text readable left-to-right or bottom-to-top

#### ICs (SOIC, TQFN, SOT-23)
- Pin 1 indicator visible in silkscreen
- Consistent orientation in groups
- Sufficient clearance for rework

#### ESP32-S3-WROOM Module
- Antenna facing board edge (no copper in keep-out)
- Minimum 15mm to board edge for antenna
- Ground pour under module (except antenna area)

### Pick-and-Place Machine Constraints

#### Feeder Compatibility
| Package | Tape Width | Min Qty/Reel |
|---------|------------|--------------|
| 0402 | 8mm | 10000 |
| 0603 | 8mm | 5000 |
| 0805 | 8mm | 3000 |
| SOIC-8 | 12mm | 2500 |
| SOIC-16 | 16mm | 1000 |
| TQFN-20 | 12mm | 3000 |
| ESP32 module | Tray | 50/tray |

#### Component Height
- Maximum after reflow: 25mm (machine clearance)
- Tallest SMD: ESP32-S3-WROOM (~3.5mm)
- Verify all components fit machine specs

### Fiducial Requirements

#### Global Fiducials
- **Quantity:** 3 minimum (asymmetric placement)
- **Size:** 1.0mm copper circle
- **Mask Opening:** 2.0mm diameter
- **Clearance:** 5mm from board edge
- **No silk/copper:** 3mm radius keep-out

#### Local Fiducials
- **Where:** Near fine-pitch components (ESP32, TQFN)
- **Size:** 0.5mm copper circle
- **Placement:** Within 50mm of target component

### Panel Design (if panelized)
- Tooling holes: 3.2mm at standard locations
- V-score or tab-routed
- Panel fiducials in addition to board fiducials
- Minimum 5mm between boards

## Solder Paste Considerations

### Stencil Design
| Component | Aperture Ratio | Notes |
|-----------|----------------|-------|
| 0402 | 0.66 | Reduce by 10% |
| 0603 | 0.80 | Standard |
| 0805 | 1.00 | Standard |
| TQFN (0.5mm pitch) | 0.66 | Home plate design |
| QFN exposed pad | 0.50 | Windowpane pattern |

### Stencil Thickness
- **Recommended:** 0.12mm (5 mil) for mixed assembly
- **Alternative:** 0.15mm (6 mil) if no 0402 parts

## Deliverables
- [ ] Component placement map with orientations
- [ ] Spacing verification report
- [ ] Fiducial placement drawing
- [ ] Pick-and-place coordinate file (CSV)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All SMD spacing meets minimum requirements
- Fiducials properly placed for machine vision
- Component orientations consistent and documented
- Centroid file exports correctly from KiCad

title: Hypothesis: Config losses not applied - verify optimizer respects config
description:
## Scientific Method: Hypothesis Testing

### Observation
The correlation analysis measured 16 losses, but the config only specifies 4:
- **Config specifies:** overlap, boundary, wirelength, clearance
- **Analysis measured:** alignment, group_cluster, spread, overlap, wirelength, etc.
- **Missing from analysis:** boundary, clearance

### Hypothesis
H0 (Null): The optimizer correctly applies only the losses specified in config
H1 (Alternative): The optimizer uses hardcoded/default losses regardless of config

### Experiment Design

1. **Control Test**: Run optimizer with a config that has ONLY overlap loss
   ```yaml
   losses:
     overlap:
       weight: 1.0
   ```
   
2. **Measure**: Check the loss_history.json output
   - If only 'overlap' appears → Config is respected (H0 supported)
   - If other losses appear → Config is NOT respected (H1 supported)

3. **Verification Test**: Run with a config that has overlap + boundary + wirelength
   - Verify all 3 appear in loss history

### Commands
```bash
# Create minimal config
cat > /tmp/test_overlap_only.yaml << 'EOF'
board:
  width_mm: 100.0
  height_mm: 150.0
losses:
  overlap:
    weight: 1.0
EOF

# Run optimizer
packages/temper-placer/.venv/bin/python3 -m temper_placer.cli optimize \
    pcb/temper.kicad_pcb \
    -c /tmp/test_overlap_only.yaml \
    -o /tmp/test_output.kicad_pcb \
    --epochs 10 \
    --loss-history /tmp/test_loss_history.json

# Check what losses were recorded
cat /tmp/test_loss_history.json | python3 -c 'import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(sorted(d["data_points"][-1]["breakdown"].keys()))'
```

### Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Control test executed with overlap-only config
- [ ] Loss history analyzed - document which losses appear
- [ ] Hypothesis confirmed or rejected with evidence
- [ ] If H1 confirmed: File follow-up issue to fix config application

title: Integrate agent role auto-assignment into PLAN phase
description:
## Problem

Agent dispatch (`tools/agents/auto_assign.py`) is disconnected from the PLAN phase:
1. `bd-plan` creates epic and tasks
2. Human must manually add `agent:role` labels
3. Human must manually run `auto_assign.py` to dispatch

This breaks automation and adds friction.

## Solution

Auto-assign agent roles during task creation in `execute_plan()`.

### Implementation Strategy

Use task content to infer appropriate agent role:
- Keywords like "design", "architecture" → `architect`
- Keywords like "test", "verify", "edge case" → `tester`
- Keywords like "implement", "refactor", "optimize" → `coder`
- Keywords like "security", "validate", "audit" → `security`

### Code Addition

In `plan.py`, after creating tasks:
```python
def _infer_agent_role(self, task: PlannedTask) -> Optional[str]:
    """Infer appropriate agent role from task content."""
    text = f"{task.title} {task.description}".lower()
    
    # Priority order matters - security/architect before coder
    if any(kw in text for kw in ["security", "vulnerability", "audit", "validation"]):
        return "security"
    elif any(kw in text for kw in ["design", "architecture", "pattern", "tradeoff"]):
        return "architect"
    elif any(kw in text for kw in ["test", "verify", "edge case", "coverage", "qa"]):
        return "tester"
    elif any(kw in text for kw in ["implement", "refactor", "optimize", "code", "fix"]):
        return "coder"
    
    return None  # No clear role, can be manually assigned

def execute_plan(self, plan: Plan, dry_run: bool = False) -> Dict[str, Any]:
    # ... existing code to create tasks ...
    
    # After creating each task
    for i, task in enumerate(plan.tasks, 1):
        task_id = self.create_task(task, parent_id=epic_id)
        
        if task_id:
            result["task_ids"].append(task_id)
            task_id_map[task.title] = task_id
            
            # Auto-assign agent role
            agent_role = self._infer_agent_role(task)
            if agent_role:
                print(f"  Auto-assigning to {agent_role} agent")
                self._run_bd(["update", task_id, "--label", f"agent:{agent_role}"])
```

## Optional: Auto-dispatch

Could also automatically trigger dispatch after labeling:
```python
if agent_role and not dry_run:
    # Trigger dispatch immediately
    subprocess.run(
        ["python3", "tools/agents/assign.py", task_id, agent_role],
        cwd=self.project_root
    )
```

But this might be too aggressive - better to keep the label-then-dispatch separation.

## Files to Modify

- `tools/gpbm/plan.py` (add `_infer_agent_role` method, update `execute_plan`)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Tasks automatically get `agent:role` labels based on content
- [ ] Role inference logic is sensible and tested
- [ ] Label is visible in `bd show` output
- [ ] `auto_assign.py` still works on labeled tasks
- [ ] No labels added if role can't be inferred (manual fallback)

title: Epic 6: Manufacturing Variability Model
description:
Ensure designs are manufacturable with real-world tolerances.

## Goal
Move from 'passes nominal DRC' to 'passes worst-case manufacturing'.

Current temper-placer checks nominal clearances. Real PCBs have:
- Trace width variation (±15% for fine pitch)
- Etch undercut/overetch
- Copper thickness variation
- Dielectric constant variation
- Registration errors between layers

A design that passes nominal may fail at manufacturing tolerance extremes.

## Three Levels of Variability Modeling

### Level 1: Simple Inflation (Always On)
Inflate all dimensions by worst-case tolerance:
- All clearances checked with -tolerance margin
- All trace widths checked with +tolerance inflation
- Simple, fast, conservative

```python
def inflated_clearance(nominal: float, tolerance: float = 0.1) -> float:
    '''Worst-case clearance (smaller)'''
    return nominal - tolerance

def inflated_width(nominal: float, tolerance: float = 0.1) -> float:
    '''Worst-case width (larger)'''
    return nominal + tolerance
```

### Level 2: Per-Parameter Tolerances (Detailed)
Track individual tolerances and their effects:

| Parameter | Nominal | Tolerance | Affects |
|-----------|---------|-----------|---------|
| Trace width | varies | ±15% | Clearance, resistance, current |
| Copper thickness (1oz) | 35µm | ±10% | Resistance, thermal |
| Copper thickness (2oz) | 70µm | ±10% | Resistance, thermal |
| Dielectric thickness | 0.2mm | ±10% | Impedance |
| Etch undercut | 0 | +0.05mm | Clearance |
| Layer registration | 0 | ±0.1mm | Via-to-trace |

```python
@dataclass
class ManufacturingTolerances:
    trace_width_pct: float = 0.15        # ±15%
    copper_thickness_pct: float = 0.10   # ±10%
    dielectric_thickness_pct: float = 0.10
    etch_undercut_mm: float = 0.05       # Always positive
    layer_registration_mm: float = 0.1
    drill_tolerance_mm: float = 0.05
    
    def worst_case_clearance(self, nominal: float, trace_width: float) -> float:
        '''Clearance reduced by etch undercut and trace width increase'''
        inflated_trace = trace_width * (1 + self.trace_width_pct)
        return nominal - self.etch_undercut_mm - (inflated_trace - trace_width) / 2
```

### Level 3: Monte Carlo Sampling (Comprehensive)
Generate N random samples from tolerance distributions:
- Sample each parameter from its distribution
- Check all constraints for each sample
- Report pass rate and failure modes

```python
def monte_carlo_validation(
    placement: PlacementState,
    tolerances: ManufacturingTolerances,
    n_samples: int = 1000,
    seed: int = 42
) -> MonteCarloResult:
    '''Run Monte Carlo validation'''
    rng = np.random.default_rng(seed)
    failures = []
    
    for i in range(n_samples):
        # Sample tolerances
        sample = tolerances.sample(rng)
        
        # Check all constraints with sampled tolerances
        violations = check_constraints(placement, sample)
        if violations:
            failures.append((i, violations))
    
    return MonteCarloResult(
        pass_rate=1.0 - len(failures) / n_samples,
        failures=failures,
        worst_case_margins=compute_margins(placement, tolerances)
    )
```

## Margin Reporting

Don't just report pass/fail. Report margins:

```
Clearance Analysis:
  Q1 to U_MCU: 10.2mm (required: 10.0mm, margin: 0.2mm, 2%)
  ⚠️ WARNING: Margin below 5% threshold

  DC_BUS+ to CGND: 12.5mm (required: 10.0mm, margin: 2.5mm, 25%)
  ✓ OK: Healthy margin
```

## Fab House Presets

Pre-configured tolerances for common fabs:

```yaml
fab_presets:
  jlcpcb_standard:
    trace_width_pct: 0.15
    min_trace_mm: 0.127
    min_clearance_mm: 0.127
    etch_undercut_mm: 0.05
    layer_registration_mm: 0.1
    
  jlcpcb_hdi:
    trace_width_pct: 0.10
    min_trace_mm: 0.075
    min_clearance_mm: 0.075
    etch_undercut_mm: 0.03
    layer_registration_mm: 0.05
    
  oshpark:
    trace_width_pct: 0.12
    min_trace_mm: 0.152
    min_clearance_mm: 0.152
    etch_undercut_mm: 0.04
```

## Manufacturability Score

Aggregate metric combining all margins:

```python
def manufacturability_score(placement: PlacementState, tolerances: ManufacturingTolerances) -> float:
    '''
    0.0 = not manufacturable
    1.0 = perfectly manufacturable with maximum margins
    
    Score = min(margin / threshold for all constraints)
    '''
    margins = compute_all_margins(placement, tolerances)
    return min(m.value / m.threshold for m in margins)
```

## Integration with Pipeline

1. **Pre-optimization**: Check if constraints are achievable with tolerances
2. **During optimization**: Include worst-case margins in loss function
3. **Post-optimization**: Full Monte Carlo validation
4. **Report**: Manufacturability score and margin breakdown

## Deliverables
1. Tolerance parameter definitions
2. Simple inflation model
3. Per-parameter tolerance model
4. Monte Carlo sampling framework
5. Margin reporting
6. Fab house presets
7. Manufacturability score

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Can specify 'JLCPCB standard' → applies their tolerances
- [ ] Design passes worst-case, not just nominal
- [ ] Reports quantify margins, not just binary pass/fail
- [ ] Monte Carlo gives confidence intervals
- [ ] Manufacturability score is actionable (0.8 = good, 0.5 = risky)

title: Create tools/gpbm/eco_client.py - Multi-user Eco client
description:
# Create Multi-User Eco Client

## Purpose
Create a Python client for the Eco semantic memory server that supports:
- Multiple user IDs (role-based and domain-based)
- Comprehensive search across user IDs
- Role-aware posting of reflections

## Location
`tools/gpbm/eco_client.py`

## Implementation

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Multi-user Eco client for GPBM workflow.

Supports separate user IDs for different agent roles and domains,
with comprehensive cross-user search capabilities.
"""

import json
import urllib.request
import urllib.error
from typing import Optional, List, Dict, Any
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class EcoConfig:
    base_url: str = "https://eco.bennetleff.workers.dev"
    
    # User ID hierarchy
    SHARED = "temper-shared"
    
    # Role-specific user IDs
    ROLES = {
        "architect": "temper-architect",
        "coder": "temper-coder", 
        "tester": "temper-tester",
        "human": "temper-human",
    }
    
    # Domain-specific user IDs
    DOMAINS = {
        "firmware": "temper-firmware",
        "placer": "temper-placer",
        "pcb": "temper-pcb",
    }


class EcoClient:
    """Client for Eco semantic memory with multi-user support."""
    
    def __init__(self, config: Optional[EcoConfig] = None):
        self.config = config or EcoConfig()
    
    def search(
        self,
        query: str,
        user_id: str,
        limit: int = 5,
        min_score: float = 0.7
    ) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
        """Search memories for a single user ID."""
        payload = {
            "query": query,
            "userId": user_id,
            "limit": limit,
            "minScore": min_score
        }
        
        try:
            data = json.dumps(payload).encode('utf-8')
            req = urllib.request.Request(
                f"{self.config.base_url}/memories/search",
                data=data,
                headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'},
                method='POST'
            )
            
            with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as response:
                if response.status == 200:
                    return json.load(response).get("results", [])
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Warning: Eco search failed for {user_id}: {e}")
        
        return []
    
    def search_comprehensive(
        self,
        query: str,
        role: Optional[str] = None,
        domain: Optional[str] = None,
        include_shared: bool = True,
        limit: int = 10,
        min_score: float = 0.7
    ) -> List[Dict[str, Any]]:
        """Search across multiple relevant user IDs."""
        user_ids = []
        
        if include_shared:
            user_ids.append(self.config.SHARED)
        
        if role and role in self.config.ROLES:
            user_ids.append(self.config.ROLES[role])
        
        if domain and domain in self.config.DOMAINS:
            user_ids.append(self.config.DOMAINS[domain])
        
        # Collect results from all user IDs
        all_results = []
        for user_id in user_ids:
            results = self.search(query, user_id, limit=5, min_score=min_score)
            for r in results:
                r['source_user_id'] = user_id
            all_results.extend(results)
        
        # Dedupe by content hash, keep highest score
        seen = {}
        for r in all_results:
            hash_key = r['memory']['contentHash']
            if hash_key not in seen or r['score'] > seen[hash_key]['score']:
                seen[hash_key] = r
        
        # Sort by score and limit
        return sorted(seen.values(), key=lambda x: x['score'], reverse=True)[:limit]
    
    def post(
        self,
        content: str,
        user_id: str,
        tags: Optional[List[str]] = None,
        primary_sector: str = "episodic",
        metadata: Optional[Dict[str, Any]] = None
    ) -> bool:
        """Post a memory to a specific user ID."""
        payload = {
            "content": content,
            "userId": user_id,
            "tags": tags or [],
            "primary_sector": primary_sector,
            "metadata": metadata or {}
        }
        
        try:
            data = json.dumps(payload).encode('utf-8')
            req = urllib.request.Request(
                f"{self.config.base_url}/memories",
                data=data,
                headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'},
                method='POST'
            )
            
            with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as response:
                return response.status in [200, 201]
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Error posting to Eco ({user_id}): {e}")
            return False
    
    def post_reflection(
        self,
        content: str,
        role: str,
        domain: Optional[str] = None,
        tags: Optional[List[str]] = None,
        is_shared: bool = False
    ) -> bool:
        """Post a reflection to the appropriate user ID(s)."""
        tags = tags or []
        
        # Determine user ID
        if role not in self.config.ROLES:
            print(f"Warning: Unknown role '{role}', using temper-shared")
            user_id = self.config.SHARED
        else:
            user_id = self.config.ROLES[role]
        
        # Build tags
        all_tags = tags + [role]
        if domain:
            all_tags.append(domain)
        
        # Post to role-specific
        success = self.post(content, user_id, all_tags)
        
        # Optionally post to shared
        if is_shared and success:
            shared_tags = tags + ["shared"]
            if domain:
                shared_tags.append(domain)
            self.post(content, self.config.SHARED, shared_tags)
        
        return success


# CLI interface
if __name__ == "__main__":
    import argparse
    
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Eco memory client")
    subparsers = parser.add_subparsers(dest="command")
    
    # Search command
    search_parser = subparsers.add_parser("search", help="Search memories")
    search_parser.add_argument("query", help="Search query")
    search_parser.add_argument("--role", choices=["architect", "coder", "tester", "human"])
    search_parser.add_argument("--domain", choices=["firmware", "placer", "pcb"])
    search_parser.add_argument("--limit", type=int, default=10)
    
    # Post command
    post_parser = subparsers.add_parser("post", help="Post a reflection")
    post_parser.add_argument("content", help="Memory content")
    post_parser.add_argument("--role", required=True, choices=["architect", "coder", "tester", "human"])
    post_parser.add_argument("--domain", choices=["firmware", "placer", "pcb"])
    post_parser.add_argument("--shared", action="store_true", help="Also post to shared")
    post_parser.add_argument("--tags", nargs="*", default=[])
    
    args = parser.parse_args()
    client = EcoClient()
    
    if args.command == "search":
        results = client.search_comprehensive(
            args.query,
            role=args.role,
            domain=args.domain,
            limit=args.limit
        )
        for i, r in enumerate(results, 1):
            print(f"\n[{i}] Score: {r['score']:.2f} (from {r.get('source_user_id', 'unknown')})")
            print(f"    {r['memory']['content'][:200]}...")
    
    elif args.command == "post":
        success = client.post_reflection(
            args.content,
            role=args.role,
            domain=args.domain,
            tags=args.tags,
            is_shared=args.shared
        )
        print("Posted successfully" if success else "Post failed")
```

## Directory Structure
```
tools/gpbm/
├── __init__.py
└── eco_client.py
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] tools/gpbm/ directory created
- [ ] eco_client.py implements EcoClient class
- [ ] search_comprehensive queries multiple user IDs
- [ ] post_reflection routes to correct user ID based on role
- [ ] CLI interface works for search and post
- [ ] Handles network errors gracefully
- [ ] Deduplicates results by content hash

## Testing
```bash
# Test search
python3 tools/gpbm/eco_client.py search "boundary loss" --role architect --domain placer

# Test post
python3 tools/gpbm/eco_client.py post "TEST: Testing multi-user posting" --role coder --domain placer
```

## Files Created
- tools/gpbm/__init__.py
- tools/gpbm/eco_client.py

## Estimated Effort
3 hours

## Labels
agent:coder, domain:tooling, phase:tools

title: Verify Soft-Body Inflation Integration with Curriculum
description:
Current Status: Soft-body inflation is implemented (overlap.py:99-106) and unit tested. However, it needs to be enabled by default and integrated with curriculum.

Tasks:
1. Depends on completion of temper-gcp.2 (Enable inflation_ramp in default config)
2. Add gradient smoothness test - verify overlap loss gradient is finite and smooth during ramp transition
3. Add curriculum integration test - verify inflation_ramp works correctly with multi-phase training
4. Test edge case: very short training runs (epochs < ramp duration)

Implementation:

- Add test_inflation_curriculum_integration to test_train.py
- Verify no gradient explosion during 0.05 -> 1.0 transition

Action After Tests Pass: Close temper-ol5 as completed.

title: Epic: Optimizer Robustness - Eliminating Local Minima and Deadlocks
description: Implement advanced optimization strategies to resolve the 23% failure rate identified in seed sensitivity analysis. Focus on breaking overlap deadlocks and cluster traps via stochastic perturbations, adaptive weighting, and smarter initialization.

title: Implement test_fault_ocp_during_heating - over-current fault via mock ADC
description: Test case: In HEATING state, set mock_sm_set_dc_bus_current(55.0f) above OCP threshold (50A±5A). Call update. Verify state == STATE_FAULT. Verify fault == FAULT_OVER_CURRENT. Verify power immediately disabled. Verify PWM disabled. Verify fault logged to EEPROM.

title: Add profiling summary report
description: Automatic report at end of optimization: slowest operations, memory peaks, bottleneck analysis.

title: Implement Pin-to-Net-Centroid Attraction Loss
description: Nets pull their component pins toward a single virtual junction. Stage: Loss Function.

title: Verify high-voltage bus monitoring with isolation
description: Design and verify isolated voltage sensing for 310V DC bus monitoring. Use resistive divider + isolation amplifier or optocoupler. Verify voltage accuracy (±5%), isolation rating (>2.5kV), bandwidth for transient detection, and overvoltage/undervoltage threshold detection. Reference Lesson 10.

title: Add position perturbation option for converged placements
description:
Add an option to perturb final positions after optimization to create variation for correlation analysis.

## Problem
When the optimizer fully converges (many epochs, with heuristics), all seeds produce nearly identical final positions. This eliminates variation needed for correlation analysis.

Current workaround: Use --no-heuristics and low epochs (50), but this produces suboptimal placements.

## Proposed Solution
Add a --perturb flag to correlation_analysis.py that applies small random perturbations to final positions before routing verification.

## TDD Test Specifications

### Test 1: Perturbation creates position variation
```python
def test_perturb_positions_creates_variation():
    """
    GIVEN identical input positions for 2 seeds
    WHEN perturb_positions is called with different seeds
    THEN output positions should differ
    """
    positions = jnp.array([[50.0, 50.0], [25.0, 75.0]])
    
    perturbed_1 = perturb_positions(positions, seed=1, magnitude=2.0)
    perturbed_2 = perturb_positions(positions, seed=2, magnitude=2.0)
    
    assert not jnp.allclose(perturbed_1, perturbed_2), 'Positions should differ'
```

### Test 2: Same seed produces same perturbation (reproducibility)
```python
def test_perturb_positions_is_reproducible():
    """
    GIVEN same positions and same seed
    WHEN perturb_positions is called twice
    THEN output should be identical
    """
    positions = jnp.array([[50.0, 50.0]])
    
    result_1 = perturb_positions(positions, seed=42, magnitude=2.0)
    result_2 = perturb_positions(positions, seed=42, magnitude=2.0)
    
    assert jnp.allclose(result_1, result_2), 'Same seed should produce same result'
```

### Test 3: Perturbation respects magnitude bounds
```python
def test_perturb_positions_respects_magnitude():
    """
    GIVEN positions and magnitude=2.0
    WHEN perturb_positions is called
    THEN all perturbations should be within [-2.0, 2.0]
    """
    positions = jnp.array([[50.0, 50.0]] * 100)  # 100 components
    magnitude = 2.0
    
    perturbed = perturb_positions(positions, seed=1, magnitude=magnitude)
    delta = perturbed - positions
    
    assert jnp.all(delta >= -magnitude), 'Perturbation below -magnitude'
    assert jnp.all(delta <= magnitude), 'Perturbation above magnitude'
```

### Test 4: CLI flag is parsed correctly
```python
def test_cli_parses_perturb_flag():
    """
    GIVEN CLI args with --perturb 3.5
    WHEN args are parsed
    THEN args.perturb should be 3.5
    """
    args = parse_args(['--pcb', 'test.kicad_pcb', '--perturb', '3.5'])
    
    assert args.perturb == 3.5
```

### Test 5: Perturbation is applied in routing verification
```python
def test_routing_verification_applies_perturbation():
    """
    GIVEN a PCB path and perturb=2.0
    WHEN run_routing_verification is called
    THEN positions used for routing should differ from original by up to 2mm
    """
    # This is an integration test - verify routing uses perturbed positions
    # by checking that routing results vary with same PCB but different seeds
    result_1 = run_routing_verification(pcb_path, seed=1, perturb=2.0)
    result_2 = run_routing_verification(pcb_path, seed=2, perturb=2.0)
    
    # Results should differ due to perturbation
    assert result_1.completion_pct != result_2.completion_pct or \
           result_1.wirelength_mm != result_2.wirelength_mm
```

## Implementation

```python
def perturb_positions(positions: jnp.ndarray, seed: int, magnitude: float = 2.0) -> jnp.ndarray:
    """Add random perturbation to positions.
    
    Args:
        positions: (N, 2) array of component positions
        seed: Random seed for reproducibility
        magnitude: Max perturbation in mm (default 2mm)
    
    Returns:
        Perturbed positions with noise in range [-magnitude, magnitude]
    """
    key = jax.random.PRNGKey(seed)
    noise = jax.random.uniform(key, positions.shape, minval=-magnitude, maxval=magnitude)
    return positions + noise
```

## Files to Modify
- scripts/correlation_analysis.py
  - Add perturb_positions() function
  - Add --perturb CLI flag
  - Apply perturbation in run_routing_verification()

title: Heuristic: Connector Edge Snapping
description:
Place connectors on board edges for external accessibility.

## Problem
Connectors (power input, signal I/O, debug) must be accessible from outside enclosure:
- Must be on board edge (not internal)
- Must face outward (correct orientation)
- Must have clearance for mating connector and cables

Random placement puts connectors in board interior, requiring manual fixes.

## Approach
1. Identify connector components (by footprint type, naming, or config)
2. Classify connector type: power, signal, debug
3. Assign to appropriate edge based on type and design constraints
4. Snap position to edge with correct orientation

## Edge Assignment Strategy
- Power input: Left edge or bottom (conventional)
- Power output: Right edge or top  
- Debug (JTAG, UART): Bottom edge (accessible during development)
- User I/O: Front panel edge (application-specific)
- Thermal interface: Top edge if near heatsink

## Implementation
- Add `identify_connectors(netlist, footprints)` function
- Add `classify_connector_purpose(connector, netlist)` function
- Add `snap_to_edge(connectors, edges, board)` function
- Handle orientation (connector opening faces outward)

## Connector Identification Heuristics
- Footprint names: Conn_*, JST*, Molex*, USB*, Header*
- Reference designators: J*, P*, CON*
- Component in netlist but no pads on inner layers → likely connector

## Example for Temper
- J_DC_IN (power): Left edge, opening facing left
- J_DEBUG (UART): Bottom edge, opening facing down
- J_OUTPUT (to coil): Top edge near half-bridge
- J_TEMP (thermocouple): Right edge near sensing circuit

## Acceptance Criteria
- All connectors within 2mm of board edge
- Connector openings face outward (not inward)
- No connector-to-connector collision on same edge

title: Heuristic: Signal Flow Preservation (Left-to-Right)
description:
Arrange components following signal flow direction for readable, reviewable layouts.

## Problem
Engineers expect signals to flow in predictable directions:
- Left-to-right for main signal path
- Top-to-bottom for secondary paths
- Input on left, output on right

Random placement violates these expectations, making review and debugging harder.

## Approach
1. Identify signal chains from netlist connectivity
2. Determine chain direction (input → processing → output)
3. Assign x-coordinates based on position in chain
4. Apply to all major signal paths, not just power

## Signal Chain Examples
- Power: Input connector → Filter → Converter → Output
- Control: Sensor → ADC → MCU → Gate Driver → Power Stage
- Communication: Connector → ESD → Buffer → MCU

## Implementation
- Add `extract_signal_chains(netlist)` function
- Add `signal_flow_placement(chains, board)` function
- Allow override via constraints YAML for unconventional flows

## Example for Temper
- Left edge: DC input connector, input protection
- Left-center: Buck converter, LDO, power distribution
- Center: MCU (central coordination point)
- Right: Gate drivers, half-bridge output
- Sensors placed near what they sense (CT near power stage)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Major signal paths flow left-to-right
- No signals flowing backward for more than 1 component
- Input connectors on left or bottom, output on right or top

title: Topological phase integration with optimizer
description:
Integrate topological phase into the main optimizer pipeline.

## Location
`packages/temper-placer/src/temper_placer/optimizer/phases.py`

## Goal
Make topological analysis a required first phase:
1. Build topological graph
2. Propagate constraints
3. Check feasibility
4. Assign zones
5. Generate initial placement
6. Pass to geometric optimizer

## Phase Architecture

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class PhaseStatus(Enum):
    PENDING = 'pending'
    RUNNING = 'running'
    SUCCESS = 'success'
    FAILED = 'failed'
    SKIPPED = 'skipped'

@dataclass
class PhaseResult:
    '''Result of a pipeline phase'''
    status: PhaseStatus
    duration_seconds: float
    state: PlacementState | None = None
    error: str | None = None
    diagnostics: list[str] = None

class TopologicalPhase:
    '''Phase 1: Topological analysis and initial placement'''
    
    def __init__(self, config: TopologicalConfig):
        self.config = config
    
    def run(self, state: PlacementState) -> PhaseResult:
        import time
        start = time.time()
        
        try:
            # Step 1: Build graph
            graph = build_topological_graph(state.pcl, state.loops)
            
            # Step 2: Propagate constraints
            propagator = ConstraintPropagator(graph)
            if not propagator.propagate():
                infeasible = propagator.get_infeasible_pairs()
                return PhaseResult(
                    status=PhaseStatus.FAILED,
                    duration_seconds=time.time() - start,
                    error=f'Infeasible constraints: {infeasible}',
                )
            
            # Step 3: Assign zones
            zone_assignment = assign_zones(state.pcl, state.component_refs)
            if zone_assignment.conflicts:
                return PhaseResult(
                    status=PhaseStatus.FAILED,
                    duration_seconds=time.time() - start,
                    error=f'Zone conflicts: {zone_assignment.conflicts}',
                )
            
            # Step 4: Generate initial positions
            positions = place_clusters(
                graph, 
                state.zones, 
                zone_assignment.assignments,
            )
            
            # Step 5: Update state
            new_state = state.with_positions(positions)
            new_state = new_state.with_zone_assignments(zone_assignment.assignments)
            new_state = new_state.with_topological_graph(graph)
            
            return PhaseResult(
                status=PhaseStatus.SUCCESS,
                duration_seconds=time.time() - start,
                state=new_state,
                diagnostics=[
                    f'Placed {len(positions)} components',
                    f'Assigned to {len(set(zone_assignment.assignments.values()))} zones',
                ],
            )
            
        except Exception as e:
            return PhaseResult(
                status=PhaseStatus.FAILED,
                duration_seconds=time.time() - start,
                error=str(e),
            )
```

## Pipeline Integration

```python
class OptimizationPipeline:
    '''Full optimization pipeline with phases'''
    
    def __init__(self, config: PipelineConfig):
        self.phases = [
            TopologicalPhase(config.topological),
            GeometricPhase(config.geometric),  # Existing optimizer
            RoutingVerificationPhase(config.routing),  # Epic 4
        ]
    
    def run(self, initial_state: PlacementState) -> PipelineResult:
        state = initial_state
        results = []
        
        for phase in self.phases:
            result = phase.run(state)
            results.append(result)
            
            if result.status == PhaseStatus.FAILED:
                return PipelineResult(
                    success=False,
                    phases=results,
                    final_state=None,
                    error=result.error,
                )
            
            state = result.state
        
        return PipelineResult(
            success=True,
            phases=results,
            final_state=state,
        )
```

## CLI Changes

```bash
# Topological phase is now automatic
temper-placer optimize input.kicad_pcb -c constraints.yaml -o output.kicad_pcb

# Skip topological phase (for debugging)
temper-placer optimize input.kicad_pcb --skip-topological

# Run only topological phase
temper-placer phase topological input.kicad_pcb -c constraints.yaml
```

## Tests Required
- Full pipeline with topological phase
- Early failure on infeasible constraints
- State passed correctly between phases
- Diagnostics captured

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Topological phase runs first
- [ ] Infeasibility fails fast with clear message
- [ ] State flows through pipeline
- [ ] Phase timing captured
- [ ] CLI integration works

title: IKW40N120H3 IGBT template
description:
## Goal
Create a component-specific template for IKW40N120H3 IGBT with thermal and electrical constraints.

## File Location
packages/temper-placer/src/temper_placer/templates/ikw40n120h3.yaml

## IKW40N120H3 Overview

The IKW40N120H3 is a 1200V/40A IGBT with integrated fast diode:
- Package: TO-247-3
- Vceo: 1200V
- Ic: 40A continuous
- Pd: 250W max
- Thermal resistance: 0.31 K/W (junction to case)
- Switching speed: ~100ns

## Template YAML

```yaml
# templates/ikw40n120h3.yaml
name: ikw40n120h3
version: "1.0"
description: "IKW40N120H3 1200V/40A IGBT with fast diode"
component_type: IGBT
manufacturer: Infineon
datasheet: "infineon-ikw40n120h3-datasheet-en.pdf"

# Package information
package:
  type: TO-247-3
  dimensions:
    width: 16.0  # mm
    height: 21.0  # mm
    mounting_hole: M3
  pins:
    1: gate
    2: collector
    3: emitter
  mounting:
    type: edge_mount
    heatsink_interface: collector_tab
    screw_torque: "0.5-0.8 Nm"

# Electrical ratings
electrical:
  vceo: 1200  # V
  ic_continuous: 40  # A at 100°C case
  ic_pulse: 120  # A
  vge_max: 20  # V
  vge_threshold: 5.8  # V typical
  
  # Gate characteristics
  gate:
    qg_total: 160  # nC
    vge_on: 15  # V recommended
    vge_off: 0  # V or -5V for faster turn-off
    rg_recommended: "3.3 to 10 ohm"

# Thermal ratings
thermal:
  tj_max: 175  # °C
  rth_jc: 0.31  # K/W junction to case
  rth_cs: 0.5  # K/W case to sink (with thermal compound)
  pd_max: 250  # W
  
  # Derating
  derating:
    - temp: 100
      ic_max: 40
    - temp: 125
      ic_max: 30
    - temp: 150
      ic_max: 20

# Safe Operating Area
soa:
  dc_limit: 40  # A at 600V
  pulse_limits:
    - duration_us: 10
      current_a: 120
      voltage_v: 600
    - duration_us: 100
      current_a: 80
      voltage_v: 600
    - duration_us: 1000
      current_a: 50
      voltage_v: 600

# PCB placement constraints
constraints:
  # Edge mounting required
  - type: on_edge
    edge: top  # Configurable
    max_distance_from_edge: 5mm
    tier: 1
    because: "TO-247 requires board edge for heatsink mounting"
    
  # Thermal spacing from sensitive components
  - type: thermal_separation
    min_distance: 30mm
    from_types: [MCU, precision_analog, temperature_sensor]
    tier: 2
    because: "IGBT heat affects temperature-sensitive components"
    
  # Creepage from control circuits
  - type: separated
    min_distance: 10mm
    from_voltage_class: low_voltage
    tier: 1
    because: "1200V rated device requires HV clearance"
    
  # Pair matching (for half-bridge)
  - type: matched_pair
    with: low_side_switch  # If this is high_side_switch
    parameter: thermal_path
    because: "Both IGBTs should have similar thermal paths to heatsink"

# Pad and copper requirements
copper:
  collector:
    pour: required
    area_min_mm2: 100  # Thermal dissipation
    copper_weight: 2oz
    via_count: 6  # Thermal vias to inner layer
    
  emitter:
    pour: required
    area_min_mm2: 50
    copper_weight: 2oz
    notes: "Switch node - also connects to gate driver GND2"
    
  gate:
    trace_width: 0.5mm
    length_max: 20mm  # To gate resistor
    notes: "Keep gate trace short, over ground plane"

# Voltage class and clearance
voltage_class: high_voltage
clearance:
  collector_to_emitter: 10mm  # On PCB
  to_low_voltage: 10mm
  working_voltage: 1200

# Thermal management
thermal_management:
  heatsink_required: true
  thermal_interface: "Thermal paste or pad"
  airflow_direction: across_fins
  
  # Operating point calculation
  operating_point:
    switching_frequency: 25000  # Hz
    duty_cycle: 0.5
    
    # Losses at typical operation
    conduction_loss_w: 30  # At 40A, 1.8V Vce_sat
    switching_loss_w: 15   # At 25kHz, 400V
    total_loss_w: 45
    
    # Temperature rise
    heatsink_rth: 0.5  # K/W
    delta_t_heatsink: 22.5  # °C above ambient
    delta_t_junction: 36  # °C above ambient (45W * 0.8 K/W total)

# Layout guidelines
guidelines:
  - "Mount with collector tab toward heatsink"
  - "Provide 6+ thermal vias under collector pad"
  - "Route emitter to gate driver GND2 as short as possible"
  - "Gate resistor within 10mm of gate pin"
  - "Use 2oz copper on power layers (L1)"
  - "Collector to emitter clearance: 10mm on PCB"
  - "Add snubber capacitor (100pF-1nF) near collector-emitter if needed"

# SPICE model reference
spice:
  model_file: "IKW40N120H3.lib"
  model_name: "IKW40N120H3"
  subcircuit: true
```

## Python Support

```python
# templates/ikw40n120h3.py
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path
import yaml

@dataclass
class IGBTTemplate:
    """IGBT component template."""
    
    name: str
    package: dict
    electrical: dict
    thermal: dict
    soa: dict
    constraints: list
    copper: dict
    voltage_class: str
    clearance: dict
    thermal_management: dict
    guidelines: list
    
    @classmethod
    def load(cls, path: Path = None) -> "IGBTTemplate":
        if path is None:
            path = Path(__file__).parent / "ikw40n120h3.yaml"
        with open(path) as f:
            data = yaml.safe_load(f)
        return cls(
            name=data.get("name", ""),
            package=data.get("package", {}),
            electrical=data.get("electrical", {}),
            thermal=data.get("thermal", {}),
            soa=data.get("soa", {}),
            constraints=data.get("constraints", []),
            copper=data.get("copper", {}),
            voltage_class=data.get("voltage_class", "high_voltage"),
            clearance=data.get("clearance", {}),
            thermal_management=data.get("thermal_management", {}),
            guidelines=data.get("guidelines", [])
        )
    
    def calculate_temperature_rise(
        self, 
        power_w: float, 
        heatsink_rth: float,
        ambient_c: float = 40.0
    ) -> dict:
        """Calculate junction temperature for given power."""
        rth_jc = self.thermal["rth_jc"]
        rth_cs = self.thermal.get("rth_cs", 0.5)
        
        total_rth = rth_jc + rth_cs + heatsink_rth
        delta_t = power_w * total_rth
        tj = ambient_c + delta_t
        
        return {
            "junction_temp_c": tj,
            "delta_t_c": delta_t,
            "total_rth_kw": total_rth,
            "within_limit": tj < self.thermal["tj_max"]
        }
    
    def get_copper_requirements(self, pin: str) -> dict:
        """Get copper pour requirements for a pin."""
        return self.copper.get(pin, {})
```

## Tests Required
- tests/templates/test_ikw40n120h3.py:
  - Test template loading
  - Test thermal calculations
  - Test SOA validation
  - Test constraint generation

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Template captures all electrical specs
- [ ] Thermal model with derating
- [ ] SOA limits defined
- [ ] Edge mounting constraints
- [ ] Copper requirements specified
- [ ] Guidelines document best practices

title: Implement ProximityLoss for component pairs
description:
Create a loss function that penalizes specific component pairs being too far apart.

## Use Cases
- Decoupling capacitor must be within 3mm of IC
- Gate driver must be within 10mm of IGBT
- Input capacitor must be near buck converter input pins

## Implementation

```python
class ProximityLoss(Loss):
    '''Penalize component pairs that exceed max distance.'''
    
    def __init__(self, proximity_rules: List[ProximityRule]):
        '''
        proximity_rules: List of (comp_a, comp_b, max_distance_mm)
        '''
        self.rules = proximity_rules
    
    def __call__(self, positions, rotations, context) -> LossResult:
        total_penalty = 0.0
        
        for rule in self.rules:
            pos_a = positions[rule.idx_a]
            pos_b = positions[rule.idx_b]
            
            distance = jnp.sqrt(jnp.sum((pos_a - pos_b) ** 2))
            
            if distance > rule.max_distance_mm:
                excess = distance - rule.max_distance_mm
                total_penalty += excess ** 2
                
        return LossResult(value=total_penalty)
```

## Pin-Aware Distance (optional enhancement)
- Use actual pin positions instead of component centers
- E.g., decoupling cap to VCC pin, not to IC center

## Testing
- Pair within distance → zero loss
- Pair too far → positive loss
- Verify pin-aware vs center-based distance

title: Fix coordinate system asymmetry in KiCad round-trip
description:
**Problem:** Import and export handle board origin asymmetrically, causing DATA CORRUPTION on round-trip.

**Import (kicad_parser.py):** Takes raw KiCad coordinates `(100, 80)`
**Export (kicad_writer.py):** Adds origin offset `x + origin[0]`

If board has `origin = (50, 30)`, a component at `(100, 80)` becomes `(150, 110)` after round-trip - **WRONG!**

**Test to verify bug:**
```python
# Parse PCB, immediately write it back, parse again
# Coordinates should match - currently they don't
```

**Fix options:**
1. Subtract origin on import, add on export (normalize to origin-relative)
2. Don't modify coordinates at all (pass through as-is)
3. Store original origin and use consistently

**Files:** 
- temper-placer/src/temper_placer/io/kicad_parser.py
- temper-placer/src/temper_placer/io/kicad_writer.py
**Risk:** HIGH - Exported PCB files have wrong component positions

title: Loop visualization in HTML reports
description:
Add loop visualization to temper-placer HTML reports. ## Location `packages/temper-placer/src/temper_placer/visualization/loop_viz.py` ## Goal Visualize current loops overlaid on component placement: - Color-coded loop paths - Loop area display (actual vs max allowed) - Warning indicators for exceeded limits ## Visualization Features ### 1. Loop Path Rendering ```python def render_loop_path( loop: Loop, placements: dict[str, Placement], color: str, ) -> str: '''Generate SVG path for a loop''' points = [] for pin in loop.pins: component = placements.get(pin.component_ref) if component: # Get pin location in placed coordinates pin_pos = component.get_pin_position(pin.pin_name) points.append(pin_pos) # Close the loop if points: points.append(points[0]) # Generate SVG polyline path_data = ' '.join(f'{x},{y}' for x, y in points) return f''' ''' ``` ### 2. Loop Area Calculation and Display ```python def calculate_loop_area( loop: Loop, placements: dict[str, Placement], ) -> float: '''Calculate actual loop area in mm²''' points = [] for pin in loop.pins: component = placements.get(pin.component_ref) if component: pin_pos = component.get_pin_position(pin.pin_name) points.append(pin_pos) # Shoelace formula for polygon area n = len(points) if n < 3: return 0.0 area = 0.0 for i in range(n): j = (i + 1) % n area += points[i][0] * points[j][1] area -= points[j][0] * points[i][1] return abs(area) / 2.0 def render_loop_area_label( loop: Loop, area_mm2: float, center: tuple[float, float], ) -> str: '''Render loop area label with status indicator''' status = 'ok' if area_mm2 <= loop.max_area_mm2 else 'exceeded' color = '#2ecc71' if status == 'ok' else '#e74c3c' return f''' {loop.name}: {area_mm2:.1f}mm² (max: {loop.max_area_mm2:.1f}) ''' ``` ### 3. Loop Legend Panel ```python def render_loop_legend(loops: LoopCollection) -> str: '''Render legend showing all loops with colors''' colors = { LoopType.COMMUTATION: '#e74c3c', # Red LoopType.GATE_DRIVE_HIGH: '#3498db', # Blue LoopType.GATE_DRIVE_LOW: '#2980b9', # Dark blue LoopType.BOOTSTRAP: '#f39c12', # Orange LoopType.SENSING: '#9b59b6', # Purple LoopType.DECOUPLING: '#1abc9c', # Teal } items = [] for loop in loops.loops: color = colors.get(loop.loop_type, '#95a5a6') items.append(f'''
{loop.name} [{loop.priority.value}]
''') return f'''

Current Loops

{''.join(items)}
''' ``` ### 4. Loop Summary Table ```python def render_loop_summary_table( loops: LoopCollection, placements: dict[str, Placement], ) -> str: '''Render table of all loops with metrics''' rows = [] for loop in loops.loops: area = calculate_loop_area(loop, placements) status = '✓' if area <= loop.max_area_mm2 else '✗' margin = ((loop.max_area_mm2 - area) / loop.max_area_mm2) * 100 rows.append(f''' {loop.name} {loop.loop_type.value} {loop.priority.value} {area:.1f} {loop.max_area_mm2:.1f} {margin:.0f}% {status} ''') return f''' {''.join(rows)}
Loop Type Priority Area (mm²) Max (mm²) Margin Status
''' ``` ### 5. Interactive Toggle ```javascript // Toggle loop visibility function toggleLoop(loopName) { const paths = document.querySelectorAll(`[data-loop="${loopName}"]`); paths.forEach(p => p.classList.toggle('hidden')); } // Highlight loop on hover function highlightLoop(loopName, highlight) { const paths = document.querySelectorAll(`[data-loop="${loopName}"]`); paths.forEach(p => { p.style.strokeWidth = highlight ? '1.5' : '0.5'; p.style.opacity = highlight ? '1' : '0.7'; }); } ``` ## Integration with Existing Visualization Extend `temper_placer/visualization/html_report.py`: ```python def generate_html_report( state: PlacementState, loops: LoopCollection | None = None, # ... existing params ) -> str: '''Generate HTML report with optional loop visualization''' # ... existing board/component rendering ... if loops: loop_svg = render_all_loops(loops, state.placements) loop_legend = render_loop_legend(loops) loop_table = render_loop_summary_table(loops, state.placements) else: loop_svg = loop_legend = loop_table = '' # ... assemble HTML ... ``` ## CSS Styling ```css .loop-legend { position: absolute; top: 10px; right: 10px; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.9); padding: 10px; border-radius: 4px; } .loop-legend-item { display: flex; align-items: center; gap: 8px; margin: 4px 0; } .color-box { width: 16px; height: 16px; border-radius: 2px; } .loop-summary .exceeded { background: #ffebee; } .loop-summary .ok { background: #e8f5e9; } ``` ## Tests Required - Loop path SVG generation - Area calculation (shoelace formula) - Legend rendering - Summary table with status - Integration with existing report ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Loop paths visible as dashed colored lines - [ ] Loop areas calculated and displayed - [ ] Exceeded limits clearly highlighted in red - [ ] Legend shows all loops with colors - [ ] Summary table shows all loop metrics - [ ] Interactive toggle to show/hide loops
